medications.indication_rationale: Systemic antibiotics for cellulitis; oral vs IV depends on severity assessed in person. Systemic toxicity may warrant IV therapy/admission.
medications.dose_route_frequency: e.g., cephalexin 500 mg four times daily (as per source note; severity may require IV per prescriber)

medications.indication_rationale: For cellulitis the correct advice is ELEVATION to reduce oedema; keeping the leg dependent would worsen swelling. (This contrasts with arterial ulcers, where elevation is harmful.)

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Elevate the affected leg above heart level when resting; take antibiotics evenly spaced; watch the redness against a marked/photographed border.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Elevation reduces oedema; even dosing and border monitoring track response.

patient_education_points.point: Bacteria from the insect bite have infected the deeper skin of your leg. You need to be seen in person today for antibiotics because you have a fever and chills.
Keep the leg raised above the level of your heart when resting — this reduces the swelling and pain (this is the opposite of what we'd advise for a poor-circulation ulcer).

If the redness rapidly spreads past a marked line, the pain becomes far worse than the skin looks, purple/black blisters or numb patches appear, or you become confused or dizzy — call an ambulance (000).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Erythema border vs the mark, fever/systemic response, and antibiotic response; escalate to IV if spreading or systemically worse.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Spread past the marked line, unbearable pain, purple blisters, or confusion/dizziness — emergency (000).

must_include_items: Recognise cellulitis with a systemic inflammatory response and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment for antibiotics (oral vs IV per severity)
Actively screen for and safety-net necrotising fasciitis and sepsis
Advise limb ELEVATION (not dependency) and border monitoring
acceptable_alternatives: Flucloxacillin/di-cloxacillin or other appropriate agent; IV therapy if systemically toxic
errors_of_omission: No urgent in-person assessment despite fever/rigors
errors_of_commission: Managing febrile spreading cellulitis fully remotely with routine follow-up
Reassuring without screening for necrotising features
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires urgent in-person antibiotic management PLUS a necrotising-fasciitis/sepsis safety-net AND correct ELEVATION advice. Advising dependency, or missing necrotising features, are key failures.
